equiml

  • View page source

mitigation

Base classes

class equiml.mitigation.base.FairnessMitigator[source]

Base class for all fairness mitigators.

property name: str

Mitigator name — defaults to lowercase class name.

class equiml.mitigation.base.PreProcessor[source]

Base class for pre-processing fairness mitigators.

Pre-processors modify training data before model fitting. They operate on features X, labels y, and sensitive attributes.

abstractmethod fit(X, y, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Fit the mitigator on training data.

Parameters:

  • X (np.ndarray) – Feature matrix of shape (n_samples, n_features).

  • y (np.ndarray) – Ground truth labels of shape (n_samples,).

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

  • **kwargs – Additional method-specific parameters.

Returns:

The fitted mitigator (self).

Return type:

PreProcessor

fit_transform(X, y, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Fit and transform in one step.

Parameters:

  • X (np.ndarray) – Feature matrix.

  • y (np.ndarray) – Ground truth labels.

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

Returns:

Transformed data.

Return type:

PreProcessingResult

abstractmethod transform(X, y, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Apply the mitigation to training data.

Parameters:

  • X (np.ndarray) – Feature matrix of shape (n_samples, n_features).

  • y (np.ndarray) – Ground truth labels of shape (n_samples,).

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

  • **kwargs – Additional method-specific parameters.

Returns:

Transformed data with X, y, weights and/or extra fields.

Return type:

PreProcessingResult

class equiml.mitigation.base.PostProcessor[source]

Base class for post-processing fairness mitigators.

Post-processors adjust model predictions after fitting. They operate on predictions and sensitive attributes only — no access to the original features X is required.

abstractmethod fit(y_true, y_pred, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Fit the mitigator on predictions.

Parameters:

  • y_true (np.ndarray) – Ground truth labels.

  • y_pred (np.ndarray) – Model predictions (labels or probabilities).

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

  • **kwargs – Additional method-specific parameters.

Returns:

The fitted mitigator (self).

Return type:

PostProcessor

abstractmethod transform(y_pred, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Adjust predictions to reduce bias.

Parameters:

  • y_pred (np.ndarray) – Model predictions (labels or probabilities).

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

  • **kwargs – Additional method-specific parameters.

Returns:

Adjusted predictions.

Return type:

np.ndarray

class equiml.mitigation.base.PreProcessingResult(X=None, y=None, weights=None, extra=<factory>)[source]

Result of a pre-processing mitigation step.

X

Transformed feature matrix. None if unchanged.

Type:

np.ndarray or None

y

Transformed labels. None if unchanged.

Type:

np.ndarray or None

weights

Sample weights. None if not applicable.

Type:

np.ndarray or None

extra

Any additional method-specific information.

Type:

dict[str, Any]

Pre-processing

class equiml.mitigation.pre.reweighting.Reweighting[source]

Reweighting for fairness-aware classification.

Assigns sample weights to training examples so that each (group, label) combination is represented according to the expected distribution under independence between the sensitive attribute and the label.

The weight for a sample with sensitive attribute S=s and label Y=y is:

\[W(s, y) = \frac{P(S=s) \cdot P(Y=y)}{P(S=s, Y=y)}\]

Under a fair model, S and Y would be independent, so the joint probability would equal the product of marginals. The weights correct for the observed dependence.

References

[RW1]

Kamiran, F., & Calders, T. (2012). Data preprocessing techniques for classification without discrimination. Knowledge and Information Systems, 33(1), 1-33. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10115-011-0463-8

fit(X, y, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Compute sample weights from training data.

Parameters:

  • X (np.ndarray) – Feature matrix of shape (n_samples, n_features). Not used directly — included for API consistency.

  • y (np.ndarray) – Ground truth binary labels of shape (n_samples,).

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

Returns:

The fitted mitigator (self).

Return type:

Reweighting

property name: str

Mitigator name — defaults to lowercase class name.

transform(X, y, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Apply sample weights to training data.

Parameters:

  • X (np.ndarray) – Feature matrix of shape (n_samples, n_features).

  • y (np.ndarray) – Ground truth labels of shape (n_samples,).

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

Returns:

Same X and y with computed sample weights.

Return type:

PreProcessingResult

class equiml.mitigation.pre.wasserstein_repair.WassersteinRepair(repair_type='geometric', lambda_=1.0, method='quantile', weights=(0.5, 0.5), random_state=None)[source]

Fair data repair using optimal transport theory.

Transforms numerical features so that their conditional distributions given the sensitive attribute are mapped towards a common barycenter distribution, reducing disparate impact while preserving rank ordering.

Two methods are available:

  • method='quantile': Operates feature-by-feature using empirical quantile functions. No additional dependencies required. Corresponds to the 1D repair of [FFM15] and the geometric repair of [GGL19].

  • method='ot': Uses optimal transport to repair all features jointly in the multidimensional case. Requires the POT package (pip install equiml[ot]). Corresponds to [GGL19].

Three repair types are available via repair_type:

  • 'total': Full repair towards the Wasserstein barycenter. Guarantees statistical parity but may reduce accuracy (lambda_=1).

  • 'geometric': Partial repair along the Wasserstein geodesic. Trade-off between fairness and accuracy controlled by lambda_.

  • 'random': Random repair as in [GGL19]. Better control of total variation distance. Only available with method='ot'.

Parameters:

  • repair_type (str, default='geometric') – Type of repair: ‘total’, ‘geometric’, or ‘random’.

  • lambda (float, default=1.0) – Repair amount in [0, 1]. 0 = no repair, 1 = full repair. Only used for ‘geometric’ and ‘random’ repair types.

  • method (str, default='quantile') – Repair method: ‘quantile’ (numpy only) or ‘ot’ (requires POT).

References

[FFM15]

Feldman, M., Friedler, S.A., Moeller, J., Scheidegger, C., & Venkatasubramanian, S. (2015). Certifying and removing disparate impact. In Proceedings of KDD (pp. 259-268). https://doi.org/10.1145/2783258.2783311

[GGL19] (1,2,3)

Gordaliza, P., Del Barrio, E., Gamboa, F., & Loubes, J.M. (2019). Obtaining fairness using optimal transport theory. In Proceedings of ICML (pp. 2357-2365). https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.03195

fit(X, y, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Fit the repair on training data.

Parameters:

  • X (np.ndarray) – Feature matrix of shape (n_samples, n_features). Must contain only numerical features.

  • y (np.ndarray) – Ground truth labels. Not used — included for API consistency.

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

Returns:

The fitted mitigator (self).

Return type:

WassersteinRepair

property name: str

Mitigator name — defaults to lowercase class name.

transform(X, y, sensitive, **kwargs)[source]

Apply the repair to the feature matrix.

Parameters:

  • X (np.ndarray) – Feature matrix of shape (n_samples, n_features).

  • y (np.ndarray) – Ground truth labels.

  • sensitive (np.ndarray or list[np.ndarray]) – Sensitive attribute(s).

Returns:

Repaired feature matrix with same y and no weights.

Return type:

PreProcessingResult
